physicalDetails.detail: Tall, field-built high elf officer whose ease in armor and on horseback marks long Farstrider experience.
Carries himself with relaxed competence and a habitual half-smile that takes the edge off hard facts without blunting them.
Chapter 9 brings him back road-worn on a lathered bay, with a broken shoulder buckle saying as much about exhaustion as his face does.
Chapter 11 renders him through dispatch pressure rather than bodily presence: hard-underlined lines, impatient script, and parchment bruised by how hard he presses the warning into the page.

voiceMarkers.detail: Dry, laconic humor; undercuts politics by describing it as absurd but never stops doing the work politics requires.
Speaks to Sylvanas in compressed shorthand built on old trust and shared history.
His dry humor survives, but chapter 9 roughens it with fatigue and the impatience of a man who has spent days holding back institutions while the field keeps bleeding.
Chapter 11 compresses him into written command: no ornament, only the hard practical line that procedure is about to outrun the field unless the job is finished first.
Chapter 14 puts Lor'themar back on the line in stripped relay form: title first, then plain truth about pressure, then a last request for any reason he can spend in Silvermoon.

beliefs.belief: The Convocation must be managed, delayed, and translated if Sylvanas is going to stay where she is most useful.
Cyndia's judgment is dependable enough that a panicked border report should be taken seriously fast.
If Sylvanas is cut off now, the Convocation gains procedural cleanliness at the price of actionable knowledge.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed by chapter 9; this is the argument Lor'themar says he has been making in person.

beliefs.belief: Evidence erasure and recall pressure are now as dangerous to the investigation as the plague’s field operatives.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 11 by the dispatch reporting scrubbed sites, missing records, and political pressure from Silvermoon.

beliefs.belief: Buying noise is still worth it if noise is all the politics left between Sylvanas and a disastrous recall.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 14 when he accepts “Then buy noise” as the best shield he is going to get.

goalsAtChapterEnd.active: Spend political capital and procedural noise to cover Sylvanas's refusal for as long as he can.
goalsAtChapterEnd.underlying: Keep Silvermoon from dragging its best field commander north while the real crisis is still running south.

emotionalStateAtChapterEnd.state: Pressed thin, pragmatic, and visibly beyond any illusion that he can win this with votes alone.

decisionsThisChapter.detail: Pressed Sylvanas about the Convocation without spoiling the ease of the patrol.
Accepted the northbound split immediately once the runner's report made a political containment strategy necessary.
Intercepts the team in person rather than sending another relay because the pressure has become too immediate for distance.
Brings both Silvermoon and Dalaran pressure to the ridge in physical form and says the real line aloud instead of hiding behind the official one.
Forces the split into clarity by naming that they no longer have four women and no longer have one road.
Pushes the latest warning through Falric’s line and the relay chain, underlining the practical order to finish the work before procedure reaches it first.
Delivers the Convocation's formal recall and the ugly truth that Hearthglen has finally made the council properly afraid.
Drops title to call Sylvanas by name when he asks for a reason he can actually spend.
Accepts “buy noise” as the operating order and stays on the political shield anyway.

abilitiesDemonstrated.ability: Reads people and institutional pressure quickly, especially Sylvanas's attempts to dodge duty and Cyndia's likely reasoning.
Uses humor as pressure relief and command lubrication without losing authority.
Translates institutional pressure into operational truth fast enough to matter in the field.
Reads the camp, the missing ranger, and the state of the mission in a single sweep on arrival.
Turns bureaucratic collapse into one actionable field sentence, translating scrubbed sites and recall pressure into usable urgency.
Translates institutional panic into one clean field exchange, stripping fear, authority, and political arithmetic down to the part Sylvanas can answer on horseback.

howOthersAddressHim.speaker: Sylvanas
howOthersAddressHim.pattern: Uses his first name in familiar operational shorthand.

howHeAddressesOthers.pattern: Uses her name rather than title in private field conversation, signaling long-standing trust.
Discusses her by name as a ranger whose judgment both he and Sylvanas already know well.

bodyLanguagePatterns.detail: Rides beside Sylvanas with easy confidence, occupying silence without crowding it.
Signals agreement and understanding with very small movements rather than speeches.
Arrives carrying exhaustion in visible gear failure instead of confession, then does the hard conversational work without ceremony.
Chapter 11 leaves his body language on the page only by implication: lines underlined hard enough to bruise parchment and handwriting telling its own story before the words are finished.

knowledge.detail: Knows Sylvanas is dodging the Convocation and knows exactly where Cyndia would send a runner to catch her.
Understands instantly that a detained human mage at the ward line is both a security and political problem.
Knows the Convocation's review of Sylvanas's authority is now immediate, personal, and close to becoming recall.
Learns the Barov route evidence, the Hearthglen lead, the partial ritual data, and Lyriel's death in one ridge-side briefing.
Understands the Kirin Tor line has also degraded into procedural review language, making the two institutions mirror each other in delay.
Learns verification teams are reaching Eastweald only to find scrubbed floors, cold ash, missing records, vanished witnesses, and local lords suddenly offended by questions.
Knows recall pressure from Silvermoon is no longer distant review but an immediate threat to the field team’s freedom of action.
Now knows the Convocation's fear has outrun skepticism and turned into formal recall pressure.
Receives Sylvanas's answer as movement rather than compliance: Hearthglen held only briefly, the dead kept standing, and the south road still outranks Silvermoon review.

arc.currentPosition: Chapter 14 turns Lor'themar from steady political shield into active rearguard politics. He is still buying road for Sylvanas, but now he is doing it with fear, noise, and collapsing patience instead of routine chamber management.

arcHistory.currentPosition: Established as Sylvanas's political counterpart: the man who rides toward chambers and votes while she rides toward the actual danger.
Chapter 11 pushes Lor’themar fully into paper-war attrition. He is still the political shield, but now the shield shows through in how hard the pen bites the page.

goalsHistory.active: Ride north and contain the Convocation before the border breach turns into an uncontrolled diplomatic crisis.
goalsHistory.underlying: Protect Sylvanas's operational freedom by handling the political fight himself.

goalsHistory.active: Hold recall and procedure off long enough for the field operation to finish before institutions erase what remains of the proof.
goalsHistory.underlying: Keep buying Sylvanas road with paper and argument even as that shield thins.

emotionalStateHistory.state: Wry but alert; outwardly composed, with the ease of banter giving way to political readiness.
Off-page but visibly strained in the dispatch itself: impatient, pressed for time, and no longer pretending procedure can be reasoned into patience forever.
